terms.definition: Agentic AI refers to systems that transition from passive content generation to active goal pursuit by using reasoning, planning, and external tools. Unlike standard chatbots, these agents can independently break down a complex prompt into sub-tasks, execute them in sequence, and self-correct if the initial attempt fails.
terms.analogy: Think of the difference between a static recipe book and a professional chef. While the book just provides the text, the chef assesses the ingredients on hand, adjusts the oven temperature in real-time, and manages the entire kitchen workflow to ensure the meal is served correctly.
terms.business_value: Moves AI from a 'co-pilot' that suggests ideas to a 'digital worker' that executes end-to-end workflows. This allows enterprises to automate complex professional services like chemical process design or supply chain logistics, shifting the human role from execution to high-level oversight.

terms.definition: LRMs are a class of models designed to move beyond simple next-token prediction by incorporating 'System 2' thinking, where the model allocates extra compute time to 'deliberate' before answering. 'Balanced Thinking' optimizes this process, ensuring the model doesn't overthink simple queries (wasting money) or underthink complex ones (causing errors).
terms.analogy: It is like a high-end hybrid car with an intelligent transmission. On a flat, easy road, it cruises in fuel-saving mode; however, when it detects a steep mountain incline, it automatically downshifts and draws more power to ensure it has enough torque to reach the summit without stalling.
terms.business_value: Directly impacts the bottom line by optimizing the 'Compute-per-Task' ratio. By ensuring the AI only 'thinks' as hard as it needs to, companies can maintain high accuracy for technical audits and engineering while preventing the ballooning cloud costs associated with over-processing simple requests.

terms.term: Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for Tool Planning
terms.definition: MCTS is a search algorithm that allows an AI agent to look ahead by simulating many possible future sequences of tool usage before taking its first step. By using a 'tree' of potential outcomes, the agent can prune away low-probability paths and select the most efficient multi-step strategy for interacting with external APIs and databases.
terms.analogy: Imagine a kitchen manager who, before starting a busy dinner service, runs a dozen mental simulations of how the orders might come in. They decide exactly which pans to pre-heat and which ingredients to prep first to ensure that no matter what the customers order, the kitchen never hits a bottleneck.
terms.business_value: Reduces the risk of 'cascading failures' in autonomous systems. By allowing agents to 'foresee' the consequences of their actions within enterprise software, businesses can deploy AI to manage sensitive databases or industrial equipment with a much higher safety and success threshold.
